pattern: Since the target sum only depends on which k values you keep, not which occurrence of a repeated value, ranking indices by value first and only afterward restoring the original left-to-right order gives the largest-sum subsequence directly.
hints: You need the k largest values, but the answer must still read as a genuine subsequence -- meaning the relative left-to-right order of the elements you keep can't change.
First figure out which k indices to keep by ranking every position by its value (largest first); when values tie, it doesn't matter which occurrence you pick since the sum only cares about the value.

Once you've chosen the k indices, sort just those indices back into their original increasing order before reading off the values -- that restores the subsequence property while keeping the maximum possible sum.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, both maximal elements are the two largest values
statement example, includes a negative value forced in to hit k
statement example, a duplicate value can be picked from either occurrence
k equals the whole array length, order is unchanged
all values identical, any k of them sum the same
all negative values, must still pick the least-negative k
strictly increasing array, largest k are simply the tail

stress.description: random arrays with heavy duplication so many valid tie-breaking choices exist
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    pool = [rng.randint(-1000, 1000) for _ in range(max(1, n // 5))]
    nums = [rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]
stress.note: a small value pool sampled repeatedly forces frequent ties among the top-k values
